questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which type of magnet exhibits magnetism only while in the presence of an external magnetic field and loses that property immediately when the field is removed?
questions.hint: Think of materials that are magnetized only when near another magnet.
questions.explanation: A temporary magnet, like an iron nail, is magnetized only in the presence of an external field and loses its magnetism when that field is removed. Option A retains magnetism permanently.

source_trace.content_anchor: can be magnetized in the presence of a magnetic field. When the magnetic field is removed, these materials lose their magnetic property

explanations_tiered.definition: A temporary magnet is a material that becomes magnetized only when placed in an external magnetic field and loses that magnetism when the field is removed.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Soft iron and iron nails are common temporary magnets. They are used where magnetism is needed only temporarily, such as in electromagnet cores.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A temporary magnet is like a sponge that holds water only when dipped in it — take it out, and it's dry.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Temporary magnets have low coercivity and high permeability. When the external H field is removed, the magnetic domains randomize, resulting in near-zero remanence.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The hysteresis loop of a temporary magnet has very low remanence \(B_r\) and low coercivity \(H_c\). Soft iron exhibits this property due to mobile domain walls.
reasoning_steps: Identify key trait: magnetism only in external field.
Recognize that loss occurs when field is removed.
Select temporary magnet.
misconceptions: Thinking temporary magnets are always weak is false; they can be strong while magnetized.
Confusing temporary magnets with electromagnets — electromagnets require current, not just an external field.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Temporary magnet: magnetic only in external field. Example: iron nail.

glossary_entry.definition: Material that exhibits magnetism only when in an external magnetic field.

flashcard.front: What type of magnet loses its magnetism when the external field is removed?
flashcard.back: A temporary magnet, such as an iron nail.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Which magnet type needs an external field to stay magnetic?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: A temporary magnet; it loses magnetism when the external field is gone.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Material magnetized only in the presence of an external magnetic field.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Temporary = magnetic only with external field.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate by magnetizing a nail with a bar magnet, then showing it no longer picks up paperclips after removing the bar magnet.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Identify various types of magnets based on their properties
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Temporary magnets lose magnetism when external field is removed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: When a bar magnet is cut into two pieces, each piece becomes a complete magnet with both north and south poles. What fundamental property of magnets does this demonstrate?

options.B: Magnetic poles always exist in pairs

questions.hint: Can you ever have an isolated north pole without a south pole?
questions.explanation: Every magnet, no matter how small, has both a north and a south pole. Cutting a magnet simply creates two smaller dipoles. Option A is false; magnetic monopoles have never been observed.

source_trace.content_anchor: Magnetic poles always exist in pairs. Thus, when a magnet is cut into two pieces, both the pieces will have the North Pole and the South Pole

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic poles always exist in pairs; it is impossible to isolate a single north or south pole.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Cutting a magnet produces two smaller magnets, each with its own north and south pole. This demonstrates that monopoles do not exist in classical electromagnetism.
explanations_tiered.beginner: A magnet is like a pencil with two ends — cut it in half, and each half still has two ends.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The magnetic dipole moment is a fundamental property; cutting redistributes the domains but cannot separate poles. The divergence of B is always zero: \(\nabla \cdot \mathbf{B} = 0\).
explanations_tiered.advanced: From Gauss's law for magnetism, \(\oint \mathbf{B} \cdot d\mathbf{A} = 0\) for any closed surface, implying no magnetic monopoles exist. Cutting a magnet yields smaller dipoles, not monopoles.
reasoning_steps: Recall: every magnet has N and S poles.
Cutting separates the material but each piece still has both poles.
Conclude: poles exist only in pairs.
misconceptions: Believing the north and south ends can be separated is false; they always come together.
Thinking a very small magnet might have only one pole is incorrect.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Magnetic poles: always in pairs. No monopoles.

glossary_entry.definition: Every magnet has both a north and a south pole; they cannot be isolated.

flashcard.front: What happens when you cut a magnet in half?
flashcard.back: Each half becomes a complete magnet with both north and south poles.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Can you get a magnet with only a north pole?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: No; magnetic poles always come in pairs.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: The principle that every magnet has both a north and a south pole.
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Poles always in pairs. Cut → two magnets.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Break a magnet in class (with permission) to demonstrate this property.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Cutting always yields two magnets with both poles. Option A is false; monopoles do not exist.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: In Activity 5.1, iron filings are sprinkled over two bar magnets placed with like poles facing each other (repelling). What pattern do the iron filings form between the magnets?

options.B: Curved lines that bend outward away from the region between the poles
options.D: No pattern; filings are repelled completely

questions.hint: Like poles repel. How do field lines behave when they push away from each other?
questions.explanation: When like poles face each other, their magnetic field lines diverge (bend outward) because the fields oppose each other. Option A describes attraction (unlike poles), where lines converge.

source_trace.content_anchor: Like poles of a magnet repel each other and bringing them together causes their magnetic field lines to diverge (bend out from each other)

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic field lines diverge (spread apart) when like poles face each other due to repulsion.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Iron filings align with field lines. For repelling like poles, the lines bend outward, creating a pattern with a neutral region between the magnets.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Two north ends push the iron filings away from the middle, making curved paths that go around the sides.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The superposition of two repelling dipole fields creates a null point between them where B=0. Iron filings avoid this region, forming diverging loops.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The field for two identical poles separated by distance d has a saddle point at the midpoint with B=0. Field lines are hyperbolic and diverge outward, never crossing.
reasoning_steps: Identify poles: like (e.g., N-N).
Recall: like poles repel.
Field lines from each pole cannot enter the other → they bend outward.
misconceptions: Thinking repelling magnets have straight lines connecting them is wrong; that's attraction.
Believing no field exists between repelling magnets is false; there is a weak or zero point but not total absence.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Like poles → field lines diverge outward.

glossary_entry.definition: Spreading apart of magnetic field lines when like poles are brought together.

flashcard.front: What pattern do iron filings show between two repelling magnets?
flashcard.back: Curved lines that bend outward away from the region between the poles.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Do field lines converge or diverge between like poles?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: They diverge (bend outward) because like poles repel.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Pattern where magnetic field lines spread apart, indicating repulsion between like poles.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Perform Activity 5.1 in class with iron filings and two bar magnets to show the pattern visually.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw magnetic field lines around magnets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Diverging lines = repulsion. Converging lines (A) = attraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.3 The earth's magnetic field and the compass
questions.question: What is the most widely accepted theory for the origin of Earth's magnetic field?

options.A: Permanent magnetization of iron ore deposits
options.B: Movement of molten iron in the outer core
options.C: Solar wind interactions with the atmosphere
options.D: Rotation of the solid inner core only

questions.hint: Think about what is liquid and moving deep inside Earth.
questions.explanation: The dynamo theory proposes that convection currents of molten iron and nickel in Earth's outer core generate the magnetic field. Option A is insufficient; bulk core material is above the Curie temperature.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.3 The earth's magnetic field and the compass
source_trace.content_anchor: movement of molten iron in the outer core is responsible for generating Earth's magnetic field

explanations_tiered.definition: Earth's magnetic field is generated by the motion of molten iron alloys in the outer core through the dynamo effect.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Convection currents in the electrically conductive outer core, combined with Earth's rotation, create self-sustaining magnetic fields via the dynamo mechanism.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a spinning ball of liquid metal inside Earth — the moving metal acts like a giant electromagnet.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The geodynamo requires three things: conductive fluid (molten iron), convection (thermal/compositional), and rapid rotation (Coriolis effect). These generate \(\mathbf{B}\) through induction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The induction equation \(\frac{\partial \mathbf{B}}{\partial t} = \nabla \times (\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}) + \eta \nabla^2 \mathbf{B}\) describes how fluid motion \(\mathbf{v}\) amplifies the magnetic field against diffusion \(\eta\).
reasoning_steps: Identify that Earth has a liquid outer core.
Recall that moving conductive material generates magnetic fields.
Combine with rotation to get self-sustaining dynamo.
misconceptions: Thinking Earth's core is permanently magnetized like a bar magnet is false; it's too hot (above Curie temperature).
Believing the magnetic field comes from the crust is incorrect; the source is deep interior.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Earth's B field: molten iron motion in outer core.

glossary_entry.definition: Process by which Earth's magnetic field is generated by convection of molten iron in the outer core.

flashcard.back: Convection currents of molten iron in the outer core.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Where does Earth's magnetic field come from?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Movement of molten iron in the outer core.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Generation of magnetic field by fluid motion in Earth's core.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Explain that above Curie temperature (~770°C for iron), permanent magnetism is lost; so core must be a dynamo, not a permanent magnet.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain the origin of Earth's magnetic field
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Dynamo theory: molten iron motion. Option A is incorrect due to high core temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor
questions.question: An infinitely long straight wire carries a current of 6.0 A. Calculate the magnetic field at a distance of 3.0 cm from the wire. (\(\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{T·m/A}\))
questions.hint: Use \(B = \frac{\mu_0 I}{2\pi r}\); convert cm to m.
questions.explanation: \(B = \frac{(4\pi \times 10^{-7})(6.0)}{2\pi (0.030)} = \frac{24\pi \times 10^{-7}}{0.06\pi} = 4.0 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{T}\). Option A might come from using radius 6 cm instead of 3 cm.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor
source_trace.content_anchor: B = μ₀I/(2πr) calculation with I=3A, r=2cm yields 3×10⁻⁵T

explanations_tiered.definition: The magnetic field around a long straight wire is inversely proportional to distance and directly proportional to current.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Using the Biot-Savart law, B = μ₀I/(2πr). Always convert distance to meters.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Plug numbers into the formula, change cm to m, cancel π, and compute.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The integration of Biot-Savart for an infinite wire yields this result. The direction is circumferential via the right-hand rule.
reasoning_steps: Identify I = 6.0 A, r = 0.030 m.
Apply B = μ₀I/(2πr).
Cancel π and compute numeric value.
misconceptions: Forgetting to convert cm to m is a common error.
Using diameter instead of radius gives half the correct value.

glossary_entry.definition: B = μ₀I/(2πr) for an infinitely long straight wire.

flashcard.front: Calculate B at 3 cm from a 6.0 A wire.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: B at 3 cm from 6.0 A wire?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Application of B = μ₀I/(2πr) with correct unit conversion.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate magnetic field of a current-carrying conductor
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.4 Magnetic field of a current carrying conductor
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. B = (4π×10⁻⁷×6)/(2π×0.03) = 4.0×10⁻⁵ T. Option A uses r = 6 cm.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
questions.question: A proton moves perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field at speed \(v\). If the speed is doubled while the field strength remains the same, how does the magnetic force change?
questions.explanation: The magnetic force is directly proportional to speed when charge, field, and angle are constant: \(F \propto v\). Doubling v doubles the force. Option C would require doubling both v and B or squaring v.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.5 Magnetic force on a moving charge in a uniform magnetic field
source_trace.content_anchor: F = qvB sinθ; force is proportional to velocity

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic force on a moving charge is F = qvB sinθ, where v is speed, B is field strength, and θ is the angle between v and B.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For perpendicular motion (θ = 90°, sinθ = 1), F = qvB. Doubling v doubles F proportionally.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Faster moving charges are pushed harder by the magnetic field — twice the speed means twice the push.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: From F = qvB (since sin90° = 1), F ∝ v. If v → 2v, then F → 2F.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Lorentz force law \(\mathbf{F} = q\mathbf{v} \times \mathbf{B}\) is linear in v. Doubling v doubles the cross product magnitude, hence force doubles.
reasoning_steps: Write F = qvB for perpendicular case.
Identify that q and B are constant.
Doubling v means new F = q(2v)B = 2(qvB) = 2F.
misconceptions: Thinking force is proportional to v² is incorrect; that applies to kinetic energy, not magnetic force.
Believing doubling v quadruples force is wrong; that would require v² dependence.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: F ∝ v (for ⟂ motion). Double v → double F.

glossary_entry.definition: F is directly proportional to speed v when charge and field are constant.

flashcard.front: If a charge's speed doubles in a perpendicular B field, what happens to magnetic force?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: How does F change if v doubles (⟂ to B)?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnetic force is linearly proportional to charge speed when B and θ are constant.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use F = qvB to derive the relationship; avoid confusion with kinetic energy (½mv²).
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Determine magnitude and direction of magnetic force on moving charge
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. F = qvB, so v doubles → F doubles. Option C (quadruples) would require v².

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
questions.question: A wire carrying current I is placed in a uniform magnetic field B. For which orientation does the wire experience the maximum magnetic force?
questions.hint: The formula is \(F = ILB\sin\theta\). When is sinθ maximum?
questions.explanation: The magnetic force on a current-carrying wire is F = ILB sinθ. Maximum occurs when sinθ = 1, i.e., θ = 90° (wire perpendicular to field). Option A gives sin0° = 0 → zero force.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
source_trace.content_anchor: force is greatest when the conductor is at right angles to the field

explanations_tiered.definition: Maximum magnetic force on a current-carrying wire occurs when the wire is perpendicular (θ = 90°) to the magnetic field.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Force depends on sinθ, which peaks at 90°. Parallel orientation yields zero force.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You get the biggest push when the wire cuts across the magnetic field lines at a right angle.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: F = ILB sinθ; sinθ has a maximum value of 1 at θ = 90°. Therefore, perpendicular orientation maximizes force.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The vector form \(\mathbf{F} = I\mathbf{L} \times \mathbf{B}\) gives magnitude \(F = ILB\sin\theta\). The cross product is maximized when L and B are perpendicular.
reasoning_steps: Write F = ILB sinθ.
Identify that I, L, B are fixed for this comparison.
Maximize sinθ → sinθ = 1 → θ = 90°.

formula_analysis.name: Force on wire
formula_analysis.derivation: From Lorentz force on moving charges.

misconceptions: Thinking parallel gives maximum force is backwards; it gives zero.
Believing 45° gives more force than 90° is false because sin45° ≈ 0.71 < 1.

glossary_entry.definition: Perpendicular orientation (θ = 90°) between wire and field yields greatest force.

flashcard.front: When is magnetic force on a current-carrying wire maximum?
flashcard.back: When the wire is perpendicular (90°) to the magnetic field.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What orientation gives maximum magnetic force on a wire?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Orientation where sinθ = 1, giving F = ILB.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Demonstrate with a wire loop in a magnetic field, showing maximum deflection at 90°.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: State relationship between magnetic force, current, and magnetic field
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.6 Magnetic force on a current carrying conductor
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. F = ILB sinθ; maximum at θ = 90° (sin90° = 1). Option A gives zero force.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.section: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel current carrying conductors
questions.question: Two long parallel wires carry currents of 5.0 A and 8.0 A in opposite directions. What is the nature of the magnetic force between them?

options.A: Attractive, with magnitude proportional to product of currents
options.B: Repulsive, with magnitude proportional to product of currents
options.D: Zero force because currents are different

questions.hint: Recall the rule for parallel currents: same direction → attract; opposite → repel.
questions.explanation: Parallel wires with currents in opposite directions experience a repulsive force. The force magnitude is proportional to I₁I₂, as given by \(F/L = \mu_0 I_1 I_2/(2\pi d)\). Option A describes same-direction currents.

source_trace.section_anchor: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel current carrying conductors
source_trace.content_anchor: If the two wires carry currents in opposite directions, they repel each other

explanations_tiered.definition: Parallel wires carrying currents in opposite directions repel each other with force per unit length \(F/L = \mu_0 I_1 I_2/(2\pi d)\).
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The direction of force depends on current direction: same → attract, opposite → repel. Magnitude depends on both currents and separation.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the currents run opposite ways, the wires push apart like two people trying to walk past each other in a narrow hallway.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Using the right-hand rule: wire 1's B field circles. For opposite currents, the force on wire 2 is away from wire 1 → repulsion.
explanations_tiered.advanced: From \(F/L = \frac{\mu_0 I_1 I_2}{2\pi d}\), the sign of the force (attractive or repulsive) is determined by the relative signs of the currents. Opposite signs → repulsion.
reasoning_steps: Identify current directions: opposite.
Recall rule: opposite → repulsive.
Magnitude depends on I₁ × I₂ regardless of direction sign.

formula_analysis.name: Force per unit length between parallel wires
formula_analysis.derivation: From B = μ₀I₁/(2πd) and F = I₂LB.

misconceptions: Thinking different magnitudes cancel force is false; force magnitude is product, not difference.
Believing opposite currents attract is the reverse of correct rule.

glossary_entry.definition: Force pushing parallel wires apart when currents flow in opposite directions.

flashcard.front: Do parallel wires with opposite currents attract or repel?
flashcard.back: They repel, because the magnetic fields cause a force away from each other.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Opposite currents in parallel wires: attract or repel?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Repel, with force proportional to I₁×I₂.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Parallel wires repel when currents flow in opposite directions.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Use the right-hand rule to derive both cases. Students often memorize but forget why.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Explain how parallel wires carrying currents can attract or repel
publishing_metadata.workbook_section: 5.7 Magnetic force between two parallel conductors
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Opposite directions → repulsion, magnitude ∝ I₁I₂. Option A describes same-direction attraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: Which medical imaging technique uses very strong magnetic fields to create detailed images of soft tissues, bones, and organs?
questions.hint: The name of the technology includes the word 'Magnetic'.
questions.explanation: MRI uses powerful magnetic fields to align hydrogen nuclei in the body, then uses radio waves to generate signals for image construction. Option A (X-ray) uses ionizing radiation, not magnetic fields.

source_trace.content_anchor: MRIs use powerful magnetic fields to generate a radar-like radio signal from inside the body

explanations_tiered.definition: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) uses strong magnetic fields and radio waves to produce detailed images of internal body structures.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: MRI exploits the magnetic properties of hydrogen nuclei (protons) in water and fat. The strong B field aligns them, and radio pulses create detectable signals.
explanations_tiered.beginner: MRI uses a giant magnet to take pictures of the inside of your body without using X-rays or surgery.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Hydrogen protons precess at the Larmor frequency in the main B field. RF pulses excite them, and relaxation times (T1, T2) generate contrast in images.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The Larmor equation \(f = \gamma B_0\) determines resonance frequency. Gradient coils create spatial encoding, allowing 3D reconstruction via Fourier transform.
reasoning_steps: Identify the technique that uses magnetic fields for imaging.
Recall that MRI stands for Magnetic Resonance Imaging.
Note that other options use radiation or sound, not magnetism.
misconceptions: Thinking MRI uses ionizing radiation is false; it uses magnetic fields and radio waves.
Believing MRI is the same as CT or X-ray is incorrect; they use different physics.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: MRI: strong magnetic fields for medical imaging.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnetic Resonance Imaging; medical imaging using strong magnetic fields and radio waves.

flashcard.front: What medical technique uses very strong magnets to image the body?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: What does MRI stand for and what does it use?
publishing_metadata.flashcard_back: Magnetic Resonance Imaging; uses strong magnetic fields.
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Imaging technique using magnetic fields and radio waves.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Discuss that MRI magnets are often 1.5 T to 3 T, much stronger than fridge magnets.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer C. MRI uses strong magnetic fields. X-rays (A) use radiation, not magnetism.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.question: What is the direction of magnetic field lines outside a bar magnet?
questions.hint: Field lines emerge from which pole and enter which pole?
questions.explanation: Outside a bar magnet, magnetic field lines originate at the north pole and terminate at the south pole. Inside the magnet, they go from south to north, forming closed loops. Option A describes inside direction.

source_trace.content_anchor: Magnetic field lines appear to emerge or start from the north pole and merge or terminate at the south pole

explanations_tiered.definition: Outside a bar magnet, magnetic field lines point from the north pole to the south pole.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Field lines are closed loops: outside N → S, inside S → N. Direction is defined by the force on a north pole.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Imagine a compass arrow: outside the magnet, the north end of the compass points away from the magnet's north pole and toward its south pole.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: By convention, field lines point away from north and toward south. A small north test pole would move from N to S outside the magnet.
explanations_tiered.advanced: The direction is defined by \(\mathbf{B} = \mu_0 (\mathbf{H} + \mathbf{M})\). Outside, H dominates and points N→S. This aligns with ∇·B=0 and ∇×H=J_free.
reasoning_steps: Recall definition: field lines go from N to S outside.
Apply to bar magnet: north pole → south pole.
misconceptions: Thinking lines go from S to N outside is wrong; that's inside the magnet.
Believing lines start and end at poles forgets they continue inside.

glossary_entry.definition: Magnetic field lines travel from north pole to south pole outside a magnet.

flashcard.front: Which way do magnetic field lines point outside a bar magnet?
flashcard.back: From north pole to south pole.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: Outside a bar magnet, do field lines go N→S or S→N?
publishing_metadata.glossary_definition: Magnetic field lines point from north to south pole outside a magnet.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Draw a bar magnet with arrows on field lines to reinforce direction.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Draw magnetic field lines around magnets
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer B. Outside: north to south. Option A is direction inside the magnet.
